hypotheses: The more a prospect’s frame is associated with risk, the lower buyers’ willingness to pay (WTP).
Buyers’ WTP is influenced by a prospect’s lowest possible outcome but not by its highest possible outcome.
Sellers’ willingness to accept (WTA) is influenced by both the lowest and highest possible outcomes but is not affected by the prospect’s frame.
The framing effect on WTP is independent of the prospect’s objective level of uncertainty.
claim.statement: Framing influences willingness to pay but not willingness to accept.
claim.statement_location: Title page, p. 725 (article title); reiterated p. 726, paragraph 1

data.source: Online behavioral experiments administered to Amazon Mechanical Turk participants
data.notes: Participants completed instructional manipulation checks; real and hypothetical monetary incentives were used.

method.description: Across four MTurk experiments, participants stated maximum willingness to pay or minimum willingness to accept for gift-certificate prospects whose frames (e.g., lottery, gift certificate) and monetary outcomes were systematically manipulated. Analyses compared mean valuations across framing and outcome conditions using parametric and non-parametric tests.
steps: Recruit participants on Amazon Mechanical Turk.
Randomly assign participants to framing and outcome conditions (and buyer vs. seller status where applicable).
Present incentive-compatible valuation tasks (WTP or WTA) for the framed prospect.
Include instructional manipulation and comprehension checks; exclude failures from analysis.
Aggregate data and compute descriptive statistics.
Conduct statistical tests (t-tests, ANOVAs, chi-square, Mann–Whitney) to examine effects of frame, outcome levels, and status on WTP/WTA.
method.models: Between-subjects t-tests, one-way and two/three-way ANOVAs, Mann–Whitney U tests, Kruskal–Wallis tests, chi-square tests
method.outcome_variable: Monetary valuation (WTP or WTA in US dollars)
method.independent_variables: Frame label (gift certificate, voucher, lottery ticket, raffle, coin flip, gamble), endowment status (buyer vs. seller), outcome structure (low/high monetary amounts), objective uncertainty (certain vs. risky)
method.control_variables: None explicitly modeled; demographic information collected but not used as controls

results.summary: Across experiments, framing a prospect with terms highly associated with risk (lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble) substantially reduced buyers’ WTP but had no reliable effect on sellers’ WTA. Buyers were sensitive to the prospect’s lowest possible outcome, whereas sellers considered both low and high outcomes. The framing effect persisted even when objective uncertainty was removed and accounted for observed instances of the Uncertainty Effect.

numerical_results.direction: frame reduced WTP but not WTA

numerical_results.outcome_name: Main effect of frame on WTP – Experiment 3

metadata.original_paper_title: Framing Influences Willingness to Pay but Not Willingness to Accept
